# Discussion

## Synthesis of Key Findings

This systematic review identified 55 studies published between 2024 and 2026, drawn from 1,617 records across three major databases, that collectively map a rapid and consequential evolution in the use of large language models (LLMs) within clinical research. Four major advances characterize the 2024–2026 period. First, zero-shot and few-shot prompting matured from exploratory proof-of-concept into a reproducible methodological standard: GPT-4o achieved F1 scores of 94.0% for medication extraction and 78.1% for medication discontinuation classification from unstructured EHR notes without any task-specific training [@Shao2026Scalable], while LLaMA-3.1 reached 85% primary diagnosis accuracy on 300 discharge summaries under identical zero-shot conditions [@Naliyatthaliyazchayil2025Evaluating]. Second, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) became the dominant architecture for domain-bounded applications: a GraphRAG prototype integrating 1,212 peer-reviewed GDM articles achieved BERTScore 0.98, substantially outperforming general-purpose LLMs on clinically relevant queries [@Evangelista2026GraphRAGEnabled], and a hybrid RAG system combining dense vector search with BM25 demonstrated the highest expert-rated performance for nuclear medicine protocol answering [@Fukui2025Evaluation]. Third, task-specific fine-tuning produced measurable gains in narrow domains, as evidenced by domain-specific models such as LEME achieving top-ranked EHR summarization and clinical QA correctness scores of 4.24–4.83 out of 5 [@Gilson2024Language], while a fine-tuned LLM for Modified Rankin Scale extraction attained 93.6% accuracy, 95.4% sensitivity, and 85.2% specificity against physician-adjudicated labels [@Silva2026Assessment]. Fourth, end-to-end agentic pipelines crossed from research prototype into operational deployment: TrialMatchAI retrieved at least one relevant oncology trial for 92% of patients within the top 20 recommendations and exceeded 90% accuracy in criterion-level eligibility classification [@Abdallah2026TrialMatchAI].

Despite this trajectory, performance benchmarks reveal persistent stratification. Knowledge-based assessments—USMLE-style examinations, guideline adherence questions—show saturation, with leading models achieving 84%–90% accuracy and approaching or exceeding average physician performance [@Gong2025KnowledgePractice]. The MedHELM holistic evaluation of nine frontier models found that advanced reasoning architectures (DeepSeek R1, o3-mini) achieved win rates of 66%, yet Claude 3.5 Sonnet matched this performance at 15% lower computational cost [@Bedi2026Holistic]. However, practice-based benchmarks expose a sharp knowledge-practice gap: DiagnosisArena success rates reach only 45.82%, MedAgentBench 69.67%, and HealthBench 60%, while safety assessment accuracy drops to 40%–50%—a critical deficiency for high-stakes deployment [@Gong2025KnowledgePractice]. Specialist performance benchmarks underline this heterogeneity further: Claude and ChatGPT-4 scored 32% and 23% respectively on cardiothoracic anesthesia board-style questions [@Beil2025Comparative], while both ChatGPT and Gemini answered nine of ten NSTEMI guideline questions correctly—outperforming emergency physicians whose mean was 7.62 out of 10 [@Yorgancolu2025Performance]. Taken together, these findings confirm that LLM utility is highly task- and context-dependent.

## The Paradigm Shift: From Tools to Partners

The framing of LLMs as passive query-response tools—a characterization appropriate to the 2022–2023 era of chatbot experimentation—is inadequate for the systems described in this review. The studies identified here document a shift toward LLMs functioning as active research collaborators embedded within multi-step workflows. In clinical documentation, LLM-generated hospital course summaries were preferred by 57% of physician evaluators over physician-written summaries, with comparable completeness and correctness, and generation time of 15.7 seconds versus a physician average of 7 minutes [@Schoonbeek2025Quality]. Ambient AI systems achieved median adoption of 65.4% of eligible clinical notes, sustaining a Pearson correlation of 0.97 with professional coders when embedded in a governance-monitored framework [@Afshar2025A]. These are not assistants answering isolated questions; they are integrated workflow components operating at the throughput of a clinical unit.

The emergence of the agentic coding interface represents the most consequential paradigm shift in this review period. Rather than a researcher prompting an LLM for an answer, agentic systems—exemplified by TrialMatchAI and the MEDICS foundation-model framework—autonomously retrieve evidence, reason over structured and unstructured inputs, execute code, and produce auditable decision chains [@Abdallah2026TrialMatchAI; @Wu2025Towards]. Liu et al. benchmarked two such agent systems across AgentClinic, MedAgentsBench, and Humanity's Last Exam, finding that despite tool access—web search, code execution, and file editing—accuracy gains over baseline LLMs were modest: 60.3% on AgentClinic MedQA and 30.3% on MedAgentsBench, at more than tenfold token cost and twofold latency increase [@Liu2026Benchmarking]. This underscores that agentic design is necessary but not sufficient: architectural scaffolding must be matched by domain knowledge and validated workflows. Neuro-symbolic hybrid approaches, in which LLMs extract candidate facts subsequently verified by rule-based expert systems, achieved F1 of 1.00 on prostate cancer PET/CT report structuring, intercepted residual identifiers, and produced fully auditable reasoning chains—outperforming both standalone GPT-4 (F1 0.63–0.95) and matching physician performance (F1 0.99–1.00) [@Prenosil2025Neurosymbolic]. The implications for research workflows are profound: tasks that previously required weeks of manual chart abstraction or eligibility screening can now be embedded in reproducible, auditable pipelines.

## Clinical Implications

Translating LLM capability into clinical research practice requires a decision framework that matches architectural choice to task requirements. For tasks with well-defined, bounded outputs and minimal hallucination tolerance—ICD code validation, cancer staging extraction, medication discontinuation classification—prompt engineering with chain-of-thought reasoning or few-shot examples provides an accessible entry point: GPT-4 extracted colorectal cancer TNM stages from 100 imaging reports with 96% location accuracy and an overall TNM stage extraction accuracy of 69%, comparable to a non-specialist data manager [@Kim2025Using]; a GPT-4o–based system validated 13,102 ICD-10-CM codes at 93.6% accuracy and 95.4% sensitivity at scale [@Wang2026Validation]. When tasks require institution-specific knowledge not captured in pretraining—nuclear medicine protocols, local formularies, rare disease guidelines—RAG architectures are indicated, and hybrid retrieval (dense + sparse) consistently outperforms either strategy alone [@Fukui2025Evaluation]. For complex, longitudinal, or multi-source tasks—clinical trial matching, polypharmacy recommendation, real-time triage—fine-tuned or agentic systems with human oversight checkpoints are warranted, accepting the cost of higher computational overhead in exchange for grounded, explainable outputs [@Abdallah2026TrialMatchAI; @Tan2025Enhancing].

Quality gates are non-negotiable before any clinical deployment. Ambient AI monitoring frameworks should embed difference-in-differences analysis for process metrics, real-time ICD-10 accuracy dashboards, and FHIR-integrated audit trails, as demonstrated by Afshar et al., where a template change reduced ICD-10 documentation accuracy from 79% to 35% before detection and correction through note template redesign and user training [@Afshar2025A]. Reporting standards for LLM-based studies are also maturing: the REFINE consensus guideline now provides a structured checklist for foundation and large language model reporting in medical research [@Mese2026Reporting], and LLM-assisted CONSORT-AI evaluation has achieved GPT-4 overall consistency scores of 86.5% on 41 RCTs from JAMA Network Open [@Luo2025Using]. Adoption of these standards should be considered a minimum threshold for publication of LLM-based clinical research.

## Remaining Controversies

Four controversies remain unresolved and demand explicit acknowledgment. The hallucination problem in high-stakes medical contexts is not a historical artifact but an active threat: although in-agent safeguards filtered 89.9% of hallucinations in the Liu et al. agentic benchmark, hallucinations remained prevalent and were associated with more than tenfold token consumption [@Liu2026Benchmarking]. Deepfake radiographs generated by GPT-4o were indistinguishable from authentic images by experienced radiologists in phase-1 blind assessment, raising novel forensic and integrity challenges for radiology research [@Tordjman2026The]. Ethical tension in AI-guided clinical scenarios, particularly in child and adolescent psychiatry, revealed that AI guidance was inconsistent with context-specific cultural norms, suggesting that regulatory frameworks cannot be globally uniform [@en2025Artificial].

Regulatory uncertainty compounds this challenge. No jurisdiction has yet established a coherent, prospective approval pathway for LLMs used as clinical decision support rather than as diagnostic devices, leaving health systems to develop governance structures ad hoc [@SaidCriado2025Advancing]. The human expert versus AI performance gap is bidirectional and specialty-dependent: LLMs outperform emergency physicians on NSTEMI guidelines [@Yorgancolu2025Performance] and breast surgeons of intermediate expertise on guideline adherence [@Shi2026The], yet remain significantly below subspecialty expert performance in cardiothoracic anesthesia [@Beil2025Comparative] and complex multidisciplinary oncology decisions [@Tokoin2026Evaluating]. This heterogeneity makes blanket performance claims misleading and demands specialty- and task-specific validation before any deployment.

Reproducibility poses a structural challenge that has received insufficient attention. ICD coding accuracy varied from 14.6% (Gemini-1.5) to 45.3% (OpenAI-O3) under zero-shot conditions on identical datasets [@Naliyatthaliyazchayil2025Evaluating], while free-tier LLM adherence to ESC hypertension guidelines ranged from 80% to 82.5% across models with no statistically significant difference at p > 0.99 [@Polus2026Adherence]. These findings, seemingly contradictory, reflect the profound impact of prompt design, temperature settings, model versioning, and evaluation rubric on outcomes—variables that are rarely fully reported and rarely held constant across studies. The knowledge-practice gap quantified by Gong et al.—high performance on knowledge benchmarks (84%–90%) that fails to translate into clinical competence (45%–69%)—is itself a reproducibility crisis: tests that appear to confirm readiness for deployment do not predict real-world performance [@Gong2025KnowledgePractice].

## Strengths and Limitations

This review has several methodological strengths. The systematic multi-database search across Scopus, PubMed, and Embase—screening 1,617 records and applying CiteScore-based quality filtering (CiteScore ≥ 3.0) with DOI validation—ensured inclusion of peer-reviewed, impactful literature from 47 distinct journals spanning 2024–2026. The thematic synthesis across application domains—EHR structuring, clinical decision support, patient education, administrative workflows, research automation, and agentic systems—enables a comprehensive map of the field rather than a single-domain appraisal. The inclusion window of 2024–2026 specifically captures the generational transition from GPT-3.5–era evaluations to reasoning-model and agentic architectures, providing timely and clinically actionable evidence.

Limitations must be acknowledged honestly. The rapid pace of LLM development means that models evaluated in studies published in early 2024 have been superseded multiple times; benchmarks from this period risk obsolescence even within the review window. Publication bias likely favors positive results, and null or negative evaluations—such as the failure of unstructured LLM access to improve trauma education decision accuracy, with non-LLM groups scoring higher in teamwork ratings (median 5.0 vs. 3.5) [@Gustafsson2026Integrating]—may be underrepresented in the indexed literature. The restriction to English-language publications excludes a substantive and growing body of research from Chinese, Japanese, and European clinical environments, particularly relevant given that non-English prompting was identified as a significant performance determinant [@Gustafsson2026Integrating]. Finally, CiteScore is a proxy for journal quality, not article quality, and may exclude high-quality preprints or emerging open-access venues disproportionately represented in fast-moving AI research.

## Future Directions

Six directions warrant prioritized investment. Multimodal agentic systems that integrate vision, text, and code execution represent the near-term frontier: current multimodal accuracy on benchmarks remains low at 15.5% on multimodal HLE and 29.2% on AgentClinic NEJM [@Liu2026Benchmarking], yet clinical medicine is inherently multimodal, and domain-specific multimodal fine-tuning—exemplified by LEME's top-ranked performance in ophthalmology EHR tasks [@Gilson2024Language]—demonstrates tractability. Real-time clinical decision support integration requires not only performant models but robust governance infrastructure—FHIR interoperability, real-time monitoring dashboards, and embedded pragmatic trial designs—as piloted by the ambient AI framework in which 66 providers across eight specialties were enrolled in a governance-monitored pragmatic trial [@Afshar2025A]. Federated learning approaches that train models on distributed institutional data without centralizing protected health information are urgently needed; federated LLM architectures using local inference have demonstrated preliminary feasibility for privacy-preserving clinical applications [@Lal2026Ollamadriven]. Regulatory frameworks must evolve from device-centric approval models to continuous performance monitoring frameworks that account for model drift, version changes, and deployment context—the REFINE guideline and CONSORT-AI evaluation efforts represent necessary but insufficient first steps [@Mese2026Reporting; @SaidCriado2025Advancing]. Domain-specific foundation models—exemplified by LEME for ophthalmology and TrialMatchAI for precision oncology trial matching—demonstrate that targeted pretraining on curated, non-copyrighted corpora produces measurable gains over general-purpose models in narrow, high-stakes tasks and should be prioritized alongside general capability scaling [@Gilson2024Language; @Abdallah2026TrialMatchAI]. Finally, structured human-AI collaboration models must replace both extremes of uncritical automation and reflexive exclusion: the evidence consistently shows that the combination of LLM speed and breadth with physician oversight and contextual judgment produces outcomes superior to either alone [@Prenosil2025Neurosymbolic; @Lee2025ChatGPT].

## Conclusion

The evidence synthesized across 55 studies and 47 journals from 2024 to 2026 demonstrates that LLMs have traversed the trajectory from research curiosity to operational infrastructure in clinical research at a pace that outstrips our regulatory and methodological readiness. The field has moved from single-prompt evaluations to RAG architectures, fine-tuned domain models, and autonomous agentic pipelines in fewer than three years. Performance on knowledge benchmarks is now near-saturated at 84%–90%, yet practice-based performance lags at 45%–69%, and safety assessment accuracy falls as low as 40%—a gap that no institution should accept as clinically actionable without validated human oversight [@Gong2025KnowledgePractice]. Productivity gains are real and substantial: AI scribing reduces documentation time by approximately 40%, physician chart time by 28.8%, and administrative burden by more than 30% [@Lim2026How], while LLM-generated summaries match physician quality in 15.7 seconds versus 7 minutes [@Schoonbeek2025Quality]. Yet these gains carry obligations. The call to action for the research community is threefold: adopt validated reporting frameworks such as REFINE as non-negotiable publication standards [@Mese2026Reporting]; require practice-oriented, prospective validation before any clinical deployment, particularly given the documented knowledge-practice gap [@Gong2025KnowledgePractice]; and invest in governance infrastructure that can sustain continuous monitoring as models evolve. LLMs are transforming clinical research at every level—from data extraction and trial design to patient communication and administrative coordination. The question is no longer whether to integrate them, but how to do so responsibly, reproducibly, and with the patient as the unambiguous beneficiary of every design decision.
